title: Hedgehogs Dilemma
wikitext:
[]: Hedgehogs Dilemma             Quote Post by Lurkis » Thu Oct 11, 2012 8:48 am The other day I had a rather heated argument with a friend on whether or not Asuka is truly a simple "bitch." His standpoint was that she was, she was cruel and just a bad person all around. I, on the other hand, argue that she is far more complex than that. I argued for some time that to call Asuka a 'bitch' is to lack an understanding of a persons demeanor versus their nature. On the outside, sure Asuka seems to be cruel and demented (demeanor), but the truth of the matter is she is scared, lonely, and afraid to get hurt; just as Shinji is. Once I made that connection between Asuka and Shinji, I also made the connection of Asuka's hedgehog dilemma. Where Shinji avoids getting close to people altogether due to fear, Asuka goes one step further and pushes people away before they have a chance to get close to her (with exceptions such as Kaji, c'mon we all have exceptions ;P). I am asking the forum for their views on the hedgehog dilemma's of not only Shinji and Asuka, but also the rest of the characters. As well as how do you think the various characters cope with these dilemma's.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Thu Oct 11, 2012 9:16 am Well, the hedgehog's dilemma is a central theme to the series in general. And althought this isn't brought on conversation often by the characters, they all seem to be aware to a certain extent of how and why they're distancing themselves from people even when they feel they're unable or unwilling to solve it. Aside from the ones who take a more obvious/first plane in the series (The children and Misato) The only one who's hedgehog's dilemma i can think of is Kaji: He distances himself from people by adopting a facade of reserved, self-assured cockiness. He never is really himself when he interacts with other people, just adopting some faked personality, like an actor or a jester, similar to Misato's way of distancing herself. As for how he deals with it... Well, he doesn't, he's just deppresingly aware of it. Admittedly the main thing that leads me to this interpretation of him is his scene with Misato near the end of ep 20, but surely there are more indications of this aspect of him throughout the series. That said i wouldn't be so sure about those outside/inside separations of Asuka being that black and white, nor am i too sure of exactly what you mean by "nature", you mean the enviroment they grew up in, something like that?

[]:             Quote Post by Der Kommissar » Thu Oct 11, 2012 1:09 pm She even pushes Kaji away in some respects. She certainly doesn't hash out her problems with the guy, quite the opposite: she tries to convince him she's a mature, healthy adult. Asuka's surliness is perhaps somewhat overstated to begin with.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Oct 11, 2012 2:16 pm Asuka's incredibly clingy in her own way. Part of her conflict with Shinji is that he doesn't play her game, and she sees it as a rejection rather than (understandable) confusion on his part. She's precocious and he's stunted.

[]:             Quote Post by Lurkis » Thu Oct 11, 2012 5:37 pm Dream wrote: The only one who's hedgehog's dilemma i can think of is Kaji: He distances himself from people by adopting a facade of reserved, self-assured cockiness. He never is really himself when he interacts with other people, just adopting some faked personality, like an actor or a jester, similar to Misato's way of distancing herself.

[]: Re: Hedgehogs Dilemma             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Oct 11, 2012 6:37 pm Lurkis wrote: The other day I had a rather heated argument with a friend on whether or not Asuka is truly a simple "bitch." His standpoint was that she was, she was cruel and just a bad person all around. I, on the other hand, argue that she is far more complex than that. I argued for some time that to call Asuka a 'bitch' is to lack an understanding of a persons demeanor versus their nature. On the outside, sure Asuka seems to be cruel and demented (demeanor), but the truth of the matter is she is scared, lonely, and afraid to get hurt; just as Shinji is.

[]:             Quote Post by Lurkis » Thu Oct 11, 2012 7:10 pm ^ I really like your opinions here and thank you for sharing them :3 I still think that the reason people can't get close to Asuka is because she is pushing them away. When they try to help her, she feels like she doesn't deserve it, I agree with that. In turn she lashes out at those trying to help her which I see as pushing them away. I am still working out what I think about Ritsuko, Misato, Gendo, and Kaji. Once I figure them out I'll post what I believe. In the meantime it would be great to see some views on Ritsuko and Gendo in particular. We don't seem to have nearly enough talks on those two xD

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Oct 11, 2012 7:16 pm Lurkis wrote: ^ I really like your opinions here and thank you for sharing them :3 I still think that the reason people can't get close to Asuka is because she is pushing them away. When they try to help her, she feels like she doesn't deserve it, I agree with that. In turn she lashes out at those trying to help her which I see as pushing them away.

[]:             Quote Post by Lurkis » Thu Oct 11, 2012 7:28 pm Bagheera wrote: That's 'cause they're old and creepy.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Oct 12, 2012 1:32 am Asuka was always a precocious little thing -- her resolution to be grown up and self-sufficient at age 4 shows that, as much as her early university admission. Combined with her being inducted as a pilot at that early age, she would have been hopelessly dislocated from any hope of a peer group. That alone is a recipe for someone who is in a constant state of frustration in her dealings with other people of her own age -- the DC style self loathing doesn't need to kick in until she hits her ceiling and is bettered by other ( = lesser, in Asuka-speak) people, shattering her self-image. The grown women in the cast are united in having issues with men, one way or another. Naoko's husband is conspicuous by his absence as she herself comments (well, that's probably because he committed suicide back in '99 in he face of incipient Alzheimer's) leading to Ritsu's noted wariness (until she crushes on her mother's new squeeze); Misato has her Elektra complex, and Maya we all know about.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Fri Oct 12, 2012 3:25 am Lurkis wrote: Of course as the series goes on we see her start to mentally break down along with everyone else. I have a question with this though, is it that she is just starting to break down or are we just starting to notice it as the series progresses and we learn more about her (her involvement with Rei, Magi, Instrumentality, Seele). On another note, her relationship with Gendo seems to be the most strained from the beginning as if it is the one person she can't find a perfect distance with.

[]:             Quote Post by Lurkis » Fri Oct 12, 2012 10:37 am Dream wrote: On the second thing: More than not being able to find a perfect distance with Gendo, she is bitterly aware of how manipulative and abusive Gendo's relationship towards her is, and yet she still lets herself be used to have some sort of love, even it it's a fake one (presumably). I don't know what Tines refers to with Naoko's husband killing himself because of alzheimer though, and Maya being a lesbian was never stated in the show (and, well, being as lesbian doesn't mean having problems with men) That aside, now that i think about it, almost all women in NGE indeed have some problem with the other gender (and vice-versa possibly) The only exceptions i can think of are Rei and Yui. Curiously the one who never seemed to even have a hedgehog's dilemma is Yui, although that could just be because of her distanced/enigmatic, almost godlike representation or role in the series.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Oct 12, 2012 12:37 pm Dream wrote: I don't know what Tines refers to with Naoko's husband killing himself because of alzheimer though.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Oct 16, 2012 1:28 pm One thing I'd like to note is that "she has detailed and multidimensional characterisation" doesn't equal "She's not a bad person." Example: Take Bleach's Nnoitra. Bleach has both villains who are just plain monstrous and sadistic, and people who just happen to be working for the enemy faction and will have you bawling by the time they bite the dust. Nnoitra probably falls somewhere in between. Kubo goes into great detail with his relationship with Neliel (First he says he just hates her out of pure mysogynism, but then, we see that he actually felt insulted by her attempts to protect him and his inferiority complex couldn't stomach the fact that she was way above his level and (justly!) saw herself as superior in both power and morals,) and gives him dimension, he has that "Don't you dare pity me"-complex, some bizarre mix of bloodlust and a death wish and a disillusionment with the seeming meaninglesness of the wold he lives in. Is he just a simple, mindless prick? No. Kubo took his dear time with this one. But he's still an arrogant, mysogynistic asshole with litlle regard for anyone's life, who does nice things like delightfully attempting to kill a defeated comerade and asking another how he possibly hasn't raped the prisoner yet. So, is he just a simple prick? No. Is he a complete asshole? Hell yes! Even a truly monstrous guy can be well-written and intriguing, even a detailed, multilayered character with complex inner mechanics can be a total asshole. So the very true claim that Asuka is a complex, multilayered character with a socially relevant message does not in any way disprove or prove wether or not she's cruel and selfish. Being realistic can even potentially make her more vile in the subjective perception of an audience member - Just like you might like her because you, say, lived through a traumatic divorce, experienced maternal neglect or found yourself under a lot of pressure to suceed, you might find yourself emotionally incapable of linking that person after seeing her treatment of Rei and how closely it resembled situations you know from real life if you were ever bullied in your life, no matter how much the more rational parts of you appreciate the good writing. You might like the message of the character because you dislike the character themselves... after all, she was doing something wrong, wasn't she? We're talking two very different sliding scales here. Realism/Dimension is largely unrelated to alignment/morality. Even if she has a good reason for acting the way she does, also has good traits, just wants to be loved and doesn't hurt anyone out of spite, none of this changes that she has done things that really harmed and hurt the people around her. While she is similar to Shinji in the reasons for her actions, that doesn't change the different results - You may prefer Asuka because she comes off more "cool", "dominant" or functional, but while Shinji is also deeply flawed and really needs to get it into his head that inaction can also cause damage, he is mostly benevolent - if he thinks he has a choice, he'll try to be helpful or at least to cause no damage. Not selflessly good by any means, but not more selfish than a realistic, not extraordinarily heroic individual with the typical immaturity of a fourteen-year-old. Yeah, he totally snapped in the end, but most people would after such an ordeal. . Halfway into episode 25', he was still operating under the warped logic that if he just makes everything worse, he should just stay where he is ... so no one else gets hurt. He DOES consider other people's feelings and act sympathetic, like when he wonders about Rei being all alone and her saying that she has nothing else, he spends time thinking about Misato's motivations or why he did to anger Asuka in episode 12. By contrast, the most compassionate thing Asuka ever did (not counting Rebuild) was to forgo her "need" for a visit to a fancy restaurant that was her idea in the first place. I think the following lines from the reneval live action scene say it best: "You're pretty cruel, but you get attached easily, don't you. But that's just like keeping a dog. And you're the type that recklessly and willfully causes men to misunderstand." To be fair, she acts like a normal friend around Hikari, doing her favors and comforting her, but towards everyone else, it's like she's not even aware they have feelings, too. She does questionable things like: a) Exploiting the lack of willpower/wish to please her of another person to get what is in Japan considered a borderline-sexual gesture from them, unsing pressure and provocation. ("You're just scared!") Just imagine the genders reversed! (Typical worried father: "My daughter, be wary of those boys who won't wait untill you're ready! Many of them just wanna "experiment".) Yes, she was genuinely intereste, but that doesn't change that she got what she wanted, with no regards about what Shinji wants. "Don't breathe, it's annoying!" Doesn't get more selfish than that. And then she has the nerve to complain that he didn't go further! b) Badmouth a comerade who has saved her life multiple times when he is currently MISSING IN ACTION. "It's his own fault for getting swallowed by a lovecraftian monstrosity! It's not like I provoked him, fully knowing that he doesn't have what it takes to shrug it off and not do something stupid". I'm not saying that she should have known that a little provocation would get him swallowed by Leliel, he DID get overconfident. I'm saying that badmouthing someone who's currently presumed dead to the face of two people who care about him is VERY low. c) Slutshaming. Over a perfectly monogamous relationship between adults of nearly the same age. It's just dreadful and hypocritical. d) Don't even get me STARTED on Rei. You might convince me that she just wanted all the others to love her, but not with Rei, that was cruelty. Just plain cruelty. Just because she offered to take some jobs that Asuka herself vocally refused! That poor Rei doesn't get she should be offended/is unable to retaliate only makes it worse. Doctor Pulaski, anyone? So while she is certainly not a 2D cardboard alphabitch, if someone calls her a bad person, don't assume they just misunderstand. They might understand, but decide that no, the circumstances don't justify the actions. They're not talking hot air, she is seriously callous at times, and some ppl might just decide that this outweighs her backstory, even if it does win the prize for being the most morbid in a show where everyone has some mayor trauma.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Oct 16, 2012 3:15 pm Kendrix wrote: Being realistic can even potentially make her more vile in the subjective perception of an audience member - Just like you might like her because you, say, lived through a traumatic divorce, experienced maternal neglect or found yourself under a lot of pressure to suceed, you might find yourself emotionally incapable of linking that person after seeing her treatment of Rei and how closely it resembled situations you know from real life if you were ever bullied in your life, no matter how much the more rational parts of you appreciate the good writing. You might like the message of the character because you dislike the character themselves... after all, she was doing something wrong, wasn't she?

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Oct 16, 2012 4:07 pm What the devil are you talking about? She didn't exhibit a single act of cruelty toward Rei in the whole damn show.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Oct 16, 2012 4:37 pm Bagheera wrote: Ah ah ah, you just got done telling us that realism was no excuse.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Oct 16, 2012 5:13 pm AuraTwilight wrote: Um, pardon me, what? What about her consistently dehumanizing Rei as a doll? What about the elevator scene when she slaps her? While I'm otherwise on board with your interpretation of Asuka, here, it's kind of dishonest to ignore that she very much has a hostile attitude towards Rei. It's central to their relationship.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Oct 16, 2012 6:19 pm Bagheera wrote: And you keep repeating them every time this comes up, never providing much substance for your claims beyond "because".

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Tue Oct 16, 2012 6:28 pm While the Asuka is discussion is, as always, very interesting in both sides of the debate, this is a very interesting thread premise and i wouldn't like to see it too derailed so... I wonder if you could make a connection between Gendo's (or Kaji's) Hedgehog's dilemma and Shinji's hedghehog's dilemma? The former is pretty much explicitly discussed in the series but as far as i know the latter comparison was never discussed. Plus i wonder if this could apply to the classmates (Hikari, Touji, Kensuke) as well, even if we barely know anything about them.

And then she has the nerve to complain that he didn't go further! b) Badmouth a comerade who has saved her life multiple times when he is currently MISSING IN ACTION. "It's his own fault for getting swallowed by a lovecraftian monstrosity! It's not like I provoked him, fully knowing that he doesn't have what it takes to shrug it off and not do something stupid". I'm not saying that she should have known that a little provocation would get him swallowed by Leliel, he DID get overconfident. I'm saying that badmouthing someone who's currently presumed dead to the face of two people who care about him is VERY low. c)

Article: Hedgehogs Dilemma
 a little provocation would get him swallowed by Leliel, he DID get overconfident. I'm saying that badmouthing someone who's currently presumed dead to the face of two people who care about him is VERY low. c) Slutshaming. Over a perfectly monogamous relationship between adults of nearly the same age. It's just dreadful and hypocritical. d) Don't even get me STARTED on Rei. You might convince me that she just wanted all the others to love her, but not with Rei, that was cruelty. Just plain cruelty. Just because she offered to take some jobs that Asuka herself vocally refused! That poor Rei doesn't get she should be offended/is unable to retaliate only makes it worse. Doctor Pulaski, anyone? So while she is certainly not a 2D cardboard alphabitch, if someone calls her a bad person, don't assume they just misunderstand. They might understand, but decide that no, the circumstances don't justify the actions. They're not talking hot air, she is seriously callous at times, and some ppl might just decide that this outweighs her backstory, even if it does win the prize for being the most morbid in a show where everyone has some mayor trauma.
